caveats: Iran was largely exempted from the cut given sanctions-depressed output, so this is a market-wide price effect rather than a direct Iranian-policy one; the recovery also tracks the broader COVID-19 demand rebound as economies reopened, which is a major confound.

justification: The 1997-98 Asian financial crisis (of which Korea's IMF request was a headline event) sharply reduced Asian oil demand, contributing to the global oil-price collapse of 1998 (Brent fell toward $10/barrel), which depressed Iran's oil-rent income that year.
caveats: The 1998 price collapse had multiple simultaneous causes, most notably OPEC's own late-1997 decision to raise output quotas into a weakening market; the Asian crisis is one contributing demand-side factor among several, and Korea's crisis specifically is only part of the broader regional demand shock.

justification: Reduced oil-export revenue from the 1998 price collapse, partly driven by the Asia-wide demand shock following the 1997 crisis, would have strained Iran's oil-dependent budget resources around FY1998-99.
caveats: Very indirect — the Iranian budget in this period was shaped primarily by domestic fiscal policy, subsidy commitments and OPEC supply decisions rather than the Korean crisis specifically; this link is speculative and the confidence is accordingly low.

justification: This event entry documents Turkey's experience of the 2008 Global Financial Crisis, but the crisis itself was a genuine global shock: world oil demand and prices collapsed in late 2008/2009, which cut Iran's oil-export revenue and dragged on Iran's GDP growth around 2008-2009.
caveats: The source event is specifically about Turkey's export/capital-flow collapse, not Iran; Iran's growth path in this period was also driven by domestic sanctions escalation, subsidy-reform policy, and mismanagement, so isolating the GFC's specific contribution to Iran's GDP series is speculative. Confidence kept low because the event record itself contains no Iran-specific detail.

justification: Global oil-price collapse accompanying the 2008-09 financial crisis reduced Iran's oil-export earnings, a major driver of its current account balance.
caveats: Event record is Turkey-specific in its description; Iran's current account is also heavily shaped by sanctions regimes and OPEC production quotas active in the same period, making the GFC's isolated contribution hard to separate from those domestic/geopolitical factors.

event_title: Gasoline price hike triggers nationwide protests
justification: Overnight ~200% subsidized-fuel price hike directly raised transport costs and fed through to headline CPI; Iran's WDI annual inflation jumped from 18.0% (2018) to 39.9% (2019) and the SCI general CPI index rose from 185.1 (1398) to 252.6 (1399).
caveats: 2019-2020 inflation was also driven by the broader 'maximum pressure' sanctions regime, rial collapse, and pre-existing double-digit inflation trend, so the fuel hike is one of several drivers, not the sole cause.

event_title: Gasoline price hike triggers nationwide protests
justification: The price hike was explicitly designed to cut the government's fuel-subsidy bill and fund a cash-transfer program, so it is a fiscal-policy background condition for that year's budget totals.
caveats: The annual budget law total is a nominal aggregate driven mainly by inflation and oil-revenue assumptions; the fuel-subsidy reform is a small share of the total and not separately visible in this series.

justification: Letting ~49 million Justice Share holders trade directly on the TSE/IFB injected a large new pool of retail participants and shares into the market; Iran's WDI market capitalization jumped from ~$754bn-equivalent (2019) to ~$3,804bn-equivalent (2020, constant 2015 US$) and stocks traded value rose from $98bn to $260bn over the same span.
caveats: The same year also saw the government actively encouraging retail stock investment as an inflation hedge amid rial depreciation, and a wave of state-owned enterprise IPOs, both of which independently inflated market cap and turnover; the WDI 'constant 2015 US$' conversion is also sensitive to the collapsing official/parallel exchange rate used, which can mechanically distort the dollar-denominated series.

event_title: Tehran Stock Exchange bubble peaks then bursts
lag_description: same year, effect visible over following 1-2 years
justification: The TSE index peaking above 2 million points on 9 Aug 2020 and then crashing ~40% is the direct proximate driver of the subsequent decline in Iran's WDI market-cap series from ~$3,804bn-equiv (2020) to ~$3,237bn (2021) and ~$2,882bn (2022).
caveats: WDI's annual market-cap figure is a year-end/average snapshot and cannot show the within-year peak-and-crash dynamic itself; continuing rial depreciation and 2021-22 macro instability also depressed dollar-denominated valuations independent of the equity crash.

event_title: Tehran Stock Exchange bubble peaks then bursts
justification: Gold coins are a competing rial-hedge asset for retail savers; some analysts describe capital rotating between gold and equities during 2020's speculative retail mania, so gold-coin price movements around the TSE peak/crash may partly reflect the same household risk appetite.
caveats: Gold-coin prices in rial terms are driven overwhelmingly by the exchange rate and global gold price, not by TSE-specific flows; any co-movement with the stock crash is largely coincidental rather than a direct substitution effect.

event_title: UNSC rejects US bid to extend Iran arms embargo
lag_description: precursor, effect materializes after the Oct 2020 sunset
justification: This vote set up the automatic Oct 2020 expiry of the UN conventional-arms embargo (Resolution 2231 sunset clause), the actual precondition for any subsequent change in Iran's recorded arms imports.
caveats: The vote itself changed nothing in trade flows; any visible effect belongs to the embargo's actual expiry two months later, and SIPRI trend-indicator arms-import values are a crude proxy that can be affected by data-reporting gaps as much as by policy.

lag_description: same day for FX; weeks-to-months pass-through to inflation
justification: This is a direct milestone within the exchange-rate series itself: the daily parallel-market rate in the FX chart crosses ~300,000 rials/USD right at end-September/1 October 2020 (from 291,240 on 9/26 to 299,730 on 9/30). Gold-coin prices, which are quoted in rials and track the dollar rate, moved in step, and rial depreciation is a standard driver of imported-goods inflation.
caveats: The rial's slide was a multi-month trend driven by sanctions, oil-export losses, and pandemic strain rather than a single-day shock; the '300,000' threshold is a reporting milestone within a continuous depreciation, not a discrete cause.

event_title: UN arms embargo on Iran expires
justification: Iran's SIPRI-tracked arms imports, which had been at a nominal $2-3m/year 2017-2020, rose to $21m in 2023 and $42m in 2024 after the UN embargo's legal sunset removed the multilateral prohibition (Iran also pursued deals with Russia/China in this window).
caveats: US and EU unilateral sanctions on Iranian arms trade remained in force throughout, muting the practical effect of the UN embargo's expiry; the 2021-2022 data gap makes the exact timing of the rise hard to pin down, and SIPRI trend-indicator values are not actual transaction prices.

justification: The agreement was framed as unlocking major Chinese investment in Iranian energy, transport, banking and telecoms infrastructure, which would be expected to show up as higher FDI inflows.
caveats: Iran's FDI net inflows (% of GDP) actually declined from 0.35% (2021) to 0.31% (2023) to 0.30% (2024), consistent with independent analysts' skepticism that the reported ~$400bn figure ever materialized as real investment; sanctions risk likely deterred Chinese firms from formal FDI regardless of the political agreement.

event_title: Raisi budget cuts National Development Fund savings rate
justification: Halving the mandated NDF savings share (40%→20%) directly redirected oil-revenue resources into the general budget; Iran's budget-law revenue/expenditure total rose from 24.4 quadrillion rials (FY1400/2021 bill) to 36.3 quadrillion rials (FY1401/2022 bill, the first Raisi-government budget), a 49% nominal jump.
caveats: Most of the nominal increase reflects Iran's ~40-45% annual inflation and rial depreciation raising the rial value of oil-revenue conversions, not the NDF rule change specifically; the NDF share change is one line among many budget assumptions.

justification: The eliminated preferential rate (42,000 rials/USD = 4,200 toman) is numerically the same 'official' peg value that WDI/IMF reported as Iran's official exchange rate 2020-2023 in this chart; the reform consolidated Iran's multi-tier FX system toward the market/NIMA rate.
caveats: The WDI annual 'official rate' series continues to show 42,000 through 2023 despite the reform, suggesting either a reporting lag or that this specific IMF series tracks a different official reference rate than the eliminated import-subsidy allocation; the parallel rate shows no sharp one-day reaction around 1 May 2022.

justification: Ending the subsidized import-currency rate for flour raised bread costs for bakeries, which is thematically adjacent to the government's separate wheat-procurement pricing policy toward domestic farmers.
caveats: The guaranteed wheat purchase price is a farm-gate producer price set independently of the consumer-facing import-subsidy mechanism that was eliminated; no direct causal channel connects the two, so this is speculative at best.

justification: The parallel-market rial rate rose from ~298,000/USD on 1 September 2022 to ~331,000/USD by 1-2 October, an ~11% depreciation coinciding with the eruption and spread of nationwide unrest following Amini's 16 September death.
caveats: This period also overlapped with stalled JCPOA revival talks and general sanctions-driven capital flight, which were independently pushing the rial down; disentangling the unrest's specific contribution from the ongoing negotiation failure is not possible from price data alone.

justification: The parallel rial rate fell sharply from ~493,000-557,000/USD in early March 2023 to ~445,000/USD on 11 March, a ~15-20% appreciation coinciding almost exactly with the 10 March Beijing-brokered announcement, consistent with contemporary reporting that markets read the deal as reduced regional risk and a step toward de-escalation.
caveats: The rial had already been volatile and somewhat elevated in late Feb/early March 2023 for unrelated reasons (drone attacks, domestic unrest fallout), so part of the 'rally' may be reversion from a temporary spike rather than a clean reaction to the diplomatic news.

event_title: $6bn Iranian funds unfrozen via South Korea-Qatar transfer
justification: Restoring CBI access to a multi-billion-dollar pool (even if restricted to humanitarian trade) could marginally ease FX-market sentiment; the parallel rate was broadly flat-to-mildly-declining (~500,000 to ~488,000/USD) through August 2023 around the transfer.
caveats: The funds were restricted to humanitarian goods purchases and never entered the open FX market, so any market effect would be sentiment-only; the rate's mild movement in this window is well within normal daily noise and not clearly distinguishable from no effect.

justification: The parallel rial rate rose from ~499,000/USD (5 Oct) to a local peak of ~528,000/USD (8 Oct) and stayed elevated through the announced refreezing, consistent with a general risk-off move around this period.
caveats: This window is dominated by the shock of Hamas's 7 October attack on Israel and fears of regional war spillover, which is a far larger and more plausible driver of the rial move than the comparatively small, already-unusable $6bn refreeze; treat the refreeze's specific contribution as minimal.

justification: BRICS accession was framed as a hedge against Western economic isolation, which could in principle support sentiment toward the rial.
caveats: The parallel rate shows only its normal steady gradual depreciation trend around 1 January 2024 (~506,000 to ~514,000/USD over two weeks), with no discernible break; BRICS membership conferred no immediate payments infrastructure or sanctions relief, so a lack of visible effect is expected and this record mainly documents that absence.

event_title: President Raisi dies in helicopter crash
justification: The parallel rial rate stayed in a narrow ~595,000-615,000/USD band through mid-to-late May 2024, showing no sharp break around the 19 May crash, suggesting markets read the succession as orderly given the Assembly of Experts/Guardian Council-controlled transition process.
caveats: Political-risk events can have delayed or diffuse effects not visible in daily FX noise; the absence of a sharp move does not rule out a small sustained risk-premium effect that is hard to isolate from concurrent sanctions and oil-price developments.

justification: The parallel rial rate rose from ~573,000/USD (16 July) to ~615,000/USD (2-3 August), roughly a 7% depreciation in the two weeks bracketing the runoff election, which could reflect market uncertainty about a reformist government's ability to secure sanctions relief.
caveats: This same window contains the 31 July assassination of Hamas leader Ismail Haniyeh in Tehran, a major regional-escalation shock that is a far more plausible driver of a rial sell-off than the election result itself; the two events are not separable in daily price data.

lag_description: immediate, with data gap during the war itself
justification: Parallel FX/gold-coin price reporting has a gap for 12-23 June 2025 (consistent with wartime disruption/reduced market activity), then resumes with the rial at ~829,000/USD (24 June) climbing to ~917,000/USD by 30 June, roughly a 10% depreciation from the pre-war ~835,000 level (11 June), directly tracking the war's oil-export disruption and general risk shock.
caveats: WDI/OPEC annual series (oil production, oil price) do not yet extend into 2025 and cannot show the war's direct effect on export volumes; the FX move also reflects the broader flight-to-safety and uncertainty over regime stability, not purely the physical damage to oil/gas infrastructure.

lag_description: anticipatory, building over the 30-day countdown to the snapback date
justification: The parallel rial rate rose steadily and sharply through the 30-day E3 snapback countdown, from ~940,000/USD (20 Aug, shortly before the 28 Aug trigger) to ~1,101,000/USD on the 27 Sept reinstatement date itself and on to ~1,179,000/USD by 2 October, a roughly 25% depreciation directly tracking the reimposition of UN sanctions.
caveats: This period coincides with Iran's ordinary high-inflation depreciation trend and with continued regional tensions post-Twelve-Day War, so part of the move reflects the underlying trend rather than the snapback announcement alone; the size and clarity of the move nonetheless make a direct link highly plausible.

lag_description: policy response to a prior multi-decade depreciation and inflation trend, not itself an immediate price mover
justification: The redenomination is a response to the cumulative depreciation and ~40% inflation visible in the FX, gold-coin, and CPI series (2025 WDI inflation ~42%) rather than a cause of further movement in nominal rial figures; implementation is deferred over a 2-to-5-year horizon.
caveats: Because the law only removes zeros from the currency unit and sets a multi-year transition, it should have no real economic effect on these series' underlying values; any apparent 'movement' around this date in the data is continuation of the pre-existing depreciation/inflation trend, not a redenomination effect.

event_title: Baba Gurgur oil strike opens Iraq's oil era
lag_description: same year, ramping over the following decade
justification: Iraq's OWID oil-production series is essentially zero through 1926 and begins with 0.52 (1927) rising to 1.4 (1929-1932) then jumping to 10.9 in 1934 when the Iraq Petroleum Company brought the field into full commercial production, exactly matching the event description.
caveats: None material -- this is a direct, well-documented start-of-series effect; the exact annual production figures are estimates reconstructed by OWID from historical industry sources and carry normal historical-data uncertainty.

event_title: Red Line Agreement forms the Iraq Petroleum cartel
justification: The Red Line Agreement locked in IPC's exclusive, monopoly control over further Iraqi (and former Ottoman-territory) oil development, setting the institutional framework within which Iraq's rising production through the 1930s-1950s occurred.
caveats: The agreement itself restricted competition rather than directly driving output; Iraq's production growth in this period tracks IPC's own investment pace and global demand more than the cartel agreement's terms specifically, so this is background context rather than a discrete cause.

event_title: Iraq Petroleum Company accepts 50-50 profit sharing
justification: Iraq's oil production more than doubled from 99.9 (1951) to 215.4 (1952) right around the 50-50 deal (retroactive to Jan 1951), consistent with IPC accelerating development once Iraq's government revenue take improved and cooperation was incentivized.
caveats: The same period saw a global oil-demand recovery, new pipeline capacity to the Mediterranean, and the Korean War-era commodity boom, all of which independently supported higher output; the 50-50 deal's revenue effect (cited in the event description) is better evidenced than its output effect.

event_title: 14 July Revolution overthrows the Hashemite monarchy
justification: The revolution opened a decade of nationalist economic policy (land reform, oil-concession renegotiation) that eventually culminated in 1961's Law 80 restricting IPC's concession area and, later, 1972 nationalization; Iraq's production continued rising through 1958-1960 (255 in 1957 to 552 in 1960) without an immediate disruption.
caveats: No sharp break is visible in production data at the 1958 transition itself; the revolution's main economic effects (land reform, later concession fights) are not captured by this oil-volume series and would need a fiscal or ownership-structure chart this database does not hold for that era.

event_title: Law No. 80 confiscates unexploited IPC concession areas
justification: By stripping IPC of 99.5% of its unexplored concession area and freezing new IOC exploration for a decade, Law 80 constrained the pace at which Iraq's oil output could have expanded beyond the fields IPC kept producing.
caveats: Iraq's measured oil production actually kept rising through the 1960s (552 in 1960 to over 800 by 1969) since IPC's existing producing fields were untouched; the law's effect is on forgone future capacity rather than a visible level drop, so this is a counterfactual/inferential link, not an observed break.

event_title: Iraq nationalizes the Iraq Petroleum Company
justification: State control over IPC's former assets let Iraq capture the full rent from expanding production and set its own output targets, and Iraqi production rose from about 842 (1972) to over 1,150-1,300 in 1973-75.
caveats: The concurrent 1973 OAPEC oil embargo and global price quadrupling are the dominant driver of the observed revenue surge; nationalization mainly determined who captured the windfall domestically rather than causing the volume increase by itself.

event_title: Iraq invades Iran, opening the Iran-Iraq War
justification: The eight-year war forced Iran into massive war financing (central-bank money creation, budget deficits), curtailed oil exports from the front-line Kharg terminal, disrupted trade routes, and drove military spending sharply higher, touching essentially every macro, fiscal, trade and price series for Iran across the 1980s.
caveats: The war coincided with the post-1979 Revolution's own economic disruption, nationalizations, capital flight, and separately imposed US/Western sanctions; disentangling the war's specific contribution from these simultaneous shocks is not possible from the aggregate data alone.

event_title: Iraq invades Iran, opening the Iran-Iraq War
justification: Iraqi oil production collapsed from about 1,524 in 1980 to 518 in 1981 as Iranian air strikes hit Iraq's export terminals and the Iran-Iraq pipeline was severed, a direct war effect visible in the same year as the invasion.
caveats: OPEC quota adjustments and the broader early-1980s oil glut also depressed prices and incentives industry-wide during these years.

event_title: Iraq invades Iran, opening the Iran-Iraq War
lag_description: same year, sustained through the war
justification: Iraq's arms imports roughly doubled from about $1.4bn (1979) to $2.25bn (1980) and kept climbing to a $4.5bn peak in 1984 as Gulf Arab and Soviet/Western financing sustained Iraq's war effort.
caveats: Some of this buildup was already underway before the invasion given regional tensions; the series also reflects Gulf state financial backing (loans that became a post-war debt burden) rather than purely combat consumption.

justification: Iraqi oil production collapsed from 1,225 (1990) to just 162 (1991) as the invasion triggered a global embargo and, from January 1991, the Gulf War bombing campaign against Iraqi oil infrastructure.
caveats: None material; this is a very direct and well-documented mechanism.

justification: With Iraqi and Kuwaiti barrels off the market, other OPEC producers including Iran raised output to capture higher prices and market share; Iran's production rose from 1,893 (1990) to 2,043 (1992).
caveats: Iran's production was already on a strong upward path from 1988-89 as part of its own post-Iran-Iraq-War reconstruction, so most of this increase reflects Iran's own recovery rather than a response to the Kuwait invasion specifically.

justification: Iraq's recorded battle-related deaths spike to 23,059 in 1991 as the US-led coalition's Gulf War campaign to expel Iraq from Kuwait unfolds, directly traceable to the invasion.
caveats: None material; coalition combat operations against Iraq in the 1991 Gulf War are the well-documented direct cause.

event_title: UN Security Council imposes comprehensive sanctions (Resolution 661)
justification: Iraq's merchandise exports collapsed from $10.3bn (1990) to just $377m (1991) as the comprehensive UN embargo cut off nearly all legal Iraqi trade, including oil exports, immediately after the resolution.
caveats: The same-year Gulf War military campaign and physical destruction of export infrastructure occurred simultaneously with the sanctions and independently suppressed exports.

event_title: UN Resolution 986 creates the Oil-for-Food framework
justification: Implementation was delayed until Iraq accepted the terms in late 1996, after which oil production climbed from 332 (1996) to 1,212 (1998) and 1,492 (1999), and exports rebounded from near-zero to $4.6bn (1997) and $5.5bn (1998), as UN-supervised oil sales resumed.
caveats: Production and exports remained well below pre-1990 levels throughout the program, and the ramp continued past 2000 for reasons including additional UN quota increases, so the Oil-for-Food resolution is one link in a longer recovery rather than a single discrete jump.

event_title: CPA Order 2 dissolves the Iraqi army and Ba'ath state
justification: Iraq's recorded armed forces personnel fall from 432,000 (2002-2003) to just 179,000 in 2004, consistent with the CPA's abrupt dissolution of the roughly 400,000-strong Iraqi army and security services in May 2003.
caveats: The count partly reflects measurement/reporting disruption during the occupation transition as much as the literal headcount of demobilized soldiers, and the subsequent rebuild (227,000 by 2005, back above 400,000 by 2006) reflects the newly formed Iraqi Security Forces rather than reinstatement of the old army.

event_title: CPA Order 39 opens Iraq to 100% foreign ownership
justification: Order 39's liberalization of foreign ownership rules was intended to spur private business formation in Iraq; recorded new business registrations rise from 3,842 (2006) to a mid-2000s peak before falling back with the security deterioration.
caveats: No Iraqi business-registration data exists for 2003-2005 to establish a pre-event baseline, and the 2006-era figures reflect a mix of security conditions, other CPA/post-CPA legal reforms, and reconstruction spending, not this order alone; CPA Order 39's foreign-ownership provisions were also later narrowed by the Iraqi parliament.

event_title: Paris Club agrees to cancel 80% of Iraq's debt
justification: Debt forgiveness is booked as an ODA grant-equivalent in DAC statistics; Iraq's recorded net ODA received jumps from $6.4bn (2004) to $28.5bn in 2005 as the Paris Club deal's main tranche of cancellation is implemented.
caveats: The same period also saw a surge in genuine post-invasion reconstruction aid from bilateral donors, which is combined with the debt-relief accounting in this aggregate ODA series and cannot be separately isolated here.

event_title: First post-invasion oil licensing round awards Rumaila to BP-CNPC
justification: International-operator investment in supergiant fields like Rumaila helped lift Iraqi oil production from about 1,393 (2009) to over 2,600 by 2017-18 as technical service contracts modernized production capacity.
caveats: Rumaila was only the first of several licensing rounds (2009-2010) covering multiple fields, and the broader 2007-2011 improvement in Iraqi security also enabled this investment, so the increase reflects the whole licensing program plus stabilization, not this single award.

event_title: ISIS captures Mosul; Kurdish forces seize Kirkuk's oil fields
justification: Iraq's recorded battle-related deaths surge from 1,882 (2013) to 13,168 in 2014, directly tracking ISIS's blitz capture of Mosul and subsequent territorial war across northern and western Iraq.
caveats: None material; this is a well-documented direct escalation of the same conflict the event describes.

event_title: ISIS captures Mosul; Kurdish forces seize Kirkuk's oil fields
justification: ISIS's 2014 offensive displaced an estimated 3+ million Iraqis; the internally-displaced-persons series jumps from 7,900 (2013) to 22,000 by 2015, consistent with the ongoing crisis, though the dataset has no 2014 observation to pin the exact timing.
caveats: The series measures new displacement associated with disasters broadly and continues climbing through the 2016-2018 Mosul liberation campaign, so later spikes reflect the war's later phases as much as the initial 2014 capture.

event_title: ISIS captures Mosul; Kurdish forces seize Kirkuk's oil fields
justification: Iraqi military expenditure jumps from $6.7bn (2014) to $11.2bn in 2015 as the government mobilized against ISIS with expanded Popular Mobilization Forces and coalition-supported operations.
caveats: The 2014-16 collapse in global oil prices simultaneously squeezed Iraq's oil-dependent budget, so the expenditure path also reflects fiscal constraints pulling the other direction, and the war against ISIS had multiple flashpoints beyond the initial Mosul capture.

event_title: IMF approves $5.34bn Stand-By Arrangement for Iraq
justification: Iraq's total external debt stock rises from $19.1bn (2015) to $23.4bn (2016) and $28.1bn (2017), a period spanning the IMF facility's disbursements alongside other emergency financing.
caveats: The 2014-16 oil price crash and the fiscal cost of the war against ISIS were the primary drivers of Iraq's need to borrow; the IMF SBA is one financing source among several (bilateral loans, bond issuance) contributing to the same debt build-up.

event_title: Iraqi forces retake Kirkuk from Kurdish control
justification: Battle-related deaths fall sharply from 10,248 (2017) to 1,294 in 2018, consistent with the winding-down of major combat operations as Iraqi forces completed the recapture of ISIS-held and disputed territory, including Kirkuk, in late 2017.
caveats: The Kirkuk operation was one piece of the broader anti-ISIS campaign that also included the much larger Mosul liberation (completed July 2017); the decline reflects the overall conflict's conclusion rather than the Kirkuk operation specifically.

lag_description: gradual, defines the regime for roughly the next 27 years (to 1971)
justification: Bretton Woods created the postwar dollar/gold-anchored fixed exchange-rate system and the IMF; Iran, as a founding IMF member, pegged the rial to the dollar within this framework, which is the institutional backdrop for the fixed official rate visible in this series from the mid-1940s until the system's collapse in the early 1970s.
caveats: This is a systemic, background institutional condition, not a proximate driver of any specific movement in the series. Iran's own currency law, the founding of Bank Melli/Central Bank of Iran, and domestic monetary policy set the actual peg level and timing; the parallel-market gap itself is driven by domestic scarcity and policy, not by Bretton Woods per se.

event_title: Volcker disinflation — federal funds rate peaks near 20%
lag_description: 1-3 year lag (effects visible through the 1981-86 global oil glut)
justification: The Volcker Fed's rate shock drove the 1981-82 global recession, which sharply reduced world oil demand and contributed to the 1980s oil glut and price collapse; this is a genuine global shock that forms part of the demand-side backdrop against which Iran's own oil production (charted alongside the US and other producers here) fell in this period.
caveats: For Iran specifically this is a minor contributing factor at most: the Iran-Iraq War (started 1980), the 1979 Revolution's disruption of the oil sector, and OPEC quota decisions are far larger and more direct drivers of Iran's own production trajectory in 1980-86. The US rate shock's effect on Iran's line in this chart is indirect, operating only through global demand rather than any mechanism specific to Iran.

lag_description: same year to 1 year lag (2008-09 demand collapse)
justification: TARP was the emergency response to the 2008 Global Financial Crisis, whose associated global recession crashed world oil demand and prices (from roughly $147/bbl to under $40/bbl within months); this is a genuine global shock forming background context for oil-producer output trends in this multi-country chart that includes both Iran and the USA.
caveats: Iran's oil production/exports around 2008-09 were also being shaped by tightening international sanctions and domestic field/investment constraints, which likely mattered more than the GFC-driven demand dip. TARP itself is a US financial-rescue mechanism, not an oil-market policy; its link to production volumes is indirect and mediated entirely through the broader recession it was meant to contain.

event_title: Land Reform Law first stage enacted
justification: Redistributing land from large landlords to tenant farmers reorganized agricultural production incentives and could plausibly shift agriculture's share of output over the following decade.
caveats: Series only shows the aggregate share, which is driven far more by rapid industrial/oil-sector growth (denominator effect) than by land reform itself; scholars dispute whether reform raised or fragmented productivity in the short run.

justification: Iran's first comprehensive development plan directed public and bank financing toward heavy industry and infrastructure, one driver of rising fixed investment and industrial value added through the mid-1960s.
caveats: Investment and industrial growth in this period also reflect rising oil revenue, private banking expansion, and general catch-up growth unrelated to the plan document itself.

justification: No chart in the index isolates a plausible national-level economic effect; at roughly $30M in damage the shock was too small relative to the national economy to register in any macro aggregate we hold.
caveats: A regional/local dataset (Qazvin province construction or mortality) would be needed to show this event's effect; none exists in this index.

lag_description: gradual, over a decade or more (chart starts 1976)
justification: The Literacy Corps conscripted educated youth to teach literacy in villages as a flagship White Revolution program, one of several inputs into Iran's rising adult literacy rate over subsequent decades.
caveats: The chart's earliest point is 1976, 14 years after founding, so the corps' isolated contribution cannot be separated from concurrent expansion of regular schooling, urbanization, and general economic development.

justification: The referendum bundled land redistribution, forestry nationalization, and profit-sharing into one reform package that reshaped Iran's rural economy and human-capital trajectory through the 1960s-70s, the backdrop against which these series move.
caveats: As an umbrella political event covering six separate reforms, no single chart cleanly isolates its effect from the individual measures (land reform, literacy corps, profit-sharing) or from the concurrent oil boom.

justification: As the Pahlavi state's central modernization program from 1963 onward, the White Revolution forms background context for Iran's overall growth trajectory across national-accounts series through the mid-1970s.
caveats: Extremely diffuse causal channel; oil revenue growth is a far larger and more direct driver of the same national-accounts series in this period.

justification: The 20%-of-net-profit worker-sharing scheme applied to large factories and could have affected labor relations and manufacturing investment incentives, the sector this series tracks.
caveats: No wage/labor-share series in the index covers this era to test the law's effect directly; manufacturing value-added growth was overwhelmingly driven by oil-financed import-substitution investment, not the profit-sharing rule.

justification: No chart in the index has forest-sector coverage extending back to the 1960s (forest-area and forest-rent series both begin in 1970 or later, well after nationalization and its main implementation in 1967).
caveats: Cannot separate any nationalization effect from later measurement; skip is more defensible than forcing a low-value link.

justification: These series' data points are directly enumerated by or benchmarked against the 1966 national census (Tehran population/household-size counts, and fertility/family-size statistics keyed to the 1966-1982 census cycle).
caveats: The census is a measurement event, not an economic driver -- it does not move the underlying demographic reality, only our knowledge of it, so 'causal' framing does not apply.

justification: The 1967 reform introduced a unified 10-55% progressive income-tax schedule, the structural basis for the income-tax revenue captured in these series once they begin in 1972.
caveats: No data exists between 1967 and 1972 to show the reform's immediate effect, and by 1972 surging oil revenue (not the tax reform) dominates any change in total tax-to-GDP and income-tax series.

justification: The plan explicitly shifted priority toward industry, agriculture, transport and communications services, plausibly contributing to the rise in both fixed capital formation and the services value-added share through the early 1970s.
caveats: This period overlaps rising oil revenue and the run-up to the 1973 price shock, both stronger and more direct drivers of investment and sectoral growth than the plan document itself.

justification: The Agreement raised the tax rate on oil income from 50% to 55% and posted prices by 21%, directly raising Iran's per-barrel oil revenue and hence the oil-rents share of GDP from 1971 onward.
caveats: Series begins in 1970, one year before the agreement, but subsequent annual increases scheduled through 1975 and the far larger 1973-74 shock make it hard to isolate the 1971 step alone.

justification: The Fifth Plan set ambitious investment and spending targets for FY1973-78, contributing to the run-up in fixed capital formation and government expenditure share of GDP, before being revised sharply upward within 18 months by the oil shock.
caveats: The plan was immediately overtaken and roughly doubled by the October 1973 oil-price shock, so its own independent effect (as opposed to the revised plan's) is hard to isolate in annual data.

event_title: Sale and Purchase Agreement replaces Consortium
justification: NIOC's assumption of full ownership and operational control of the oil industry, ending the 1954 Consortium structure, increased Iran's retained share of oil revenue captured in the oil-rents series.
caveats: This structural change is contemporaneous with the much larger October 1973 OPEC price shock, making the two effects very difficult to separate in annual data.

justification: OPEC price increases following the Arab oil embargo roughly quadrupled Iran's per-barrel oil revenue within a year, directly and immediately raising oil rents as a share of GDP and the government-finance series (tax/expenditure/revenue) once they begin coverage in 1972.
caveats: Government-finance series only start in 1972 so the pre-shock baseline is short; production volume itself did not fall (Iran actually raised output to capture market share), so any 'oil' effect here is on price/revenue, not physical production.

justification: The oil windfall financed a large-scale state spending and investment boom (the revised Fifth Development Plan), contributing to elevated GDP growth and fixed capital formation through 1974-76.
caveats: Multi-causal: global growth conditions, the pre-existing Fourth/Fifth Plan investment push, and credit expansion also drove this period's growth; the same windfall soon produced overheating and the 1976 credit squeeze.

justification: Absorption of the sudden oil windfall into an economy with limited absorptive capacity (ports, skilled labor, construction capacity) fed directly into Iran's mid-1970s inflation surge, prompting the 1975-77 anti-profiteering and 1977 austerity responses.
caveats: Global inflation was also elevated in this period (post-Bretton-Woods, first oil shock hit importers worldwide too), so not all of Iran's CPI rise traces to its own oil windfall specifically.

event_title: Great Civilization spending surge -- Fifth Plan revised
justification: The Shah's decision to nearly double planned investment ($36.5bn to $70bn) and raise total government spending ($44bn to $123bn) directly and sharply lifted fixed capital formation and government expenditure/consumption shares of GDP from 1974-76.
caveats: Government-finance series begin in 1972, giving only a short pre-surge baseline; the same spending surge is also the proximate cause of the 1976 credit squeeze and inflation acceleration below.

event_title: Great Civilization spending surge -- Fifth Plan revised
justification: The doubling of planned spending overwhelmed Iran's absorptive capacity (ports congested, skilled-labor shortages, construction-material bottlenecks), directly driving the inflation surge that peaked in 1976-77 and triggered both the credit squeeze and Amouzegar austerity program.
caveats: Concurrent global inflation and the earlier 1973 oil shock itself (which financed the surge) make the two hard to fully disentangle as separate inflationary impulses.

justification: No chart plausibly captures a direct economic effect of establishing a one-party political system; its main economic channel (organizing the anti-profiteering campaign) is captured separately under that campaign's own event entries.
caveats: Purely institutional/political change; any economic effect runs through the price-control campaign it enabled, already covered elsewhere.

justification: Rastakhiz Party task forces enforced price controls nationwide, intended to suppress measured inflation, though such campaigns often instead disrupt supply chains and can worsen shortages/black-market pricing.
caveats: Effect on the official CPI is theoretically ambiguous (suppressed official prices vs. supply disruption raising effective prices); the same period's inflation is also driven by the oil-boom overheating described above, making attribution to the campaign alone weak.

justification: Rising inflation combined with a deliberate Central Bank credit tightening directly choked off financing for the construction boom, sharply slowing fixed investment, broad money growth, and overall GDP growth from 1976.
caveats: Some slowdown from the extreme, unsustainable 1974-75 growth rates would likely have occurred even without deliberate tightening, simply from diminishing absorptive capacity; hard to fully separate policy-driven from capacity-driven deceleration.

justification: This series' earliest data point (1976) is drawn directly from the Fourth national census's household enumeration; the census is the data-generating event for this chart's starting benchmark.
caveats: A census is a measurement event, not an economic driver -- it documents household structure rather than causing it, so 'causal' does not apply.

justification: Continued price-control enforcement into 1977 targeted the same measured consumer-price series, amid deepening bazaari discontent ahead of the revolution.
caveats: By this point the Amouzegar austerity program (below) is a more direct and better-documented driver of disinflation; the campaign's incremental effect on top of austerity is hard to isolate.

justification: Amouzegar's mandate to curb inflation through spending cuts, halted development projects, and tightened credit directly reduced government expenditure share of GDP, investment, and growth through 1977-78.
caveats: This period is increasingly confounded by the gathering revolutionary unrest itself (strikes, capital flight, political uncertainty), which independently depressed investment and growth alongside the deliberate austerity.

justification: The austerity program's explicit goal was curbing inflation via tighter fiscal and credit policy.
caveats: Any disinflationary effect is confounded with the credit squeeze already underway since 1976 and, from late 1978, with the revolution's disruption of normal price-setting and distribution.

justification: No chart isolates a plausible national economic effect; at roughly $50M in damage the earthquake is small relative to the national economy, and it struck amid the much larger revolutionary crisis unfolding the same autumn.
caveats: Any national-level economic movement in late 1978 is overwhelmingly attributable to the oil workers' strike and revolution, not this earthquake.

justification: The general strike beginning late October 1978 directly cut oil output from roughly 6 million to 1.5 million barrels/day and shut refineries, banks, ports and factories nationwide, a documented, immediate hit to oil production/rents, exports, reserves and overall GDP growth in 1978-79.
caveats: The strike is itself both a symptom and an accelerant of the broader revolutionary crisis (event below), so its effects on 1979 aggregates are inseparable from the regime collapse that followed weeks later.

justification: The collapse of the Pahlavi government and proclamation of the Islamic Republic triggered capital flight, nationalizations, elite emigration, and the loss of the state's administrative continuity, producing a deep contraction across national-accounts and expenditure aggregates.
caveats: The subsequent US hostage crisis, sanctions, asset freezes, and the September 1980 outbreak of the Iran-Iraq War compound and eventually dominate the same series, so the revolution's isolated effect is hard to separate from these follow-on shocks.

justification: NIOC's disruption and the flight of foreign technical staff during and after the revolution kept oil output and export volumes far below pre-strike levels through 1979, directly visible in production and consumption series.
caveats: Effects overlap with the preceding oil workers' strike (already logged above) and are not separable from it in annual data.

justification: The oil-export halt and subsequent nationalization of foreign trade under the new government sharply reduced Iran's goods exports, which are overwhelmingly oil-dominated.
caveats: The relevant WDI goods-export series only begins coverage in 1976, giving a short pre-revolution baseline; later US sanctions (from November 1979) compound the same decline.

justification: Import behavior in 1979 is genuinely ambiguous: foreign-exchange scarcity and capital controls likely curtailed imports, but panic-driven stockpiling and the new government's need to substitute for lost domestic production could have pushed some import categories up in the short run.
caveats: No direct evidence in-index distinguishes these offsetting effects for 1979 specifically.

lag_description: same year, widening over following years
justification: Loss of confidence, capital flight, and the end of the Pahlavi-era managed exchange-rate regime opened a persistent gap between the official and parallel-market rial/dollar rate that the index's FX chart is explicitly built to show.
caveats: The chart's own framing (official vs. parallel gap) already anticipates this widening, so some of the apparent 'effect' reflects the chart's construction as much as the underlying shock.

justification: The new government inherited a fiscal apparatus in flux: oil-revenue collapse from the strike/export halt cut government revenue sharply, while post-revolution nationalizations and social spending commitments raised outlays, producing a fiscal position that moved on both sides of the ledger rather than in one clean direction.
caveats: Government-finance series end in 2009 for some components and have gaps; disentangling revenue-side from spending-side movements requires more granular budget data than these WDI aggregates provide.

justification: Nationalization of the banking system in June 1979 and subsequent deficit monetization contributed to accelerating money-supply and broad-money growth in the early 1980s.
caveats: Wartime financing needs from September 1980 onward are a larger and more direct driver of the same monetary expansion than the revolution's initial banking nationalization alone.

justification: Supply disruption, the collapse of the price-control apparatus, and currency depreciation contributed to accelerating consumer-price inflation in the years following the revolution.
caveats: The subsequent war economy and its own shortages/rationing are a further, larger driver of the same inflation trend from late 1980 onward, making the revolution's isolated share hard to quantify.

justification: Industrial disruption, nationalizations, and the flight of managerial/technical staff plausibly contributed to rising unemployment in the early Islamic Republic period.
caveats: Most modeled ILO labor-force series in this index only begin coverage in the 1990s, well after this period, so this link cannot actually be verified against the charts we hold and should be treated as weakly supported.

justification: Post-revolution purges of the Pahlavi-era officer corps initially disrupted and likely cut military spending, but the buildup toward and outbreak of the Iran-Iraq War in September 1980 reversed this within about 18 months into sharply higher expenditure.
caveats: The direction genuinely flips within this short window, so a single 'increase' or 'decrease' label would misrepresent the actual path; the September 1980 war outbreak is the larger, more direct driver of the eventual spending surge.

justification: The revolution triggered a large, well-documented wave of elite and middle-class emigration (the post-revolution exile population), directly reflected in migration-stock series covering this period.
caveats: Later waves of emigration were also driven by the Iran-Iraq War and subsequent political/economic crises, so not all migration-series movement in the following decades traces to 1979 specifically.

justification: Nationalization of major industries and banks, capital flight, and the flight of foreign investors sharply worsened Iran's investment climate and disrupted domestic capital-market activity from 1979.
caveats: Coverage of Iran in these WDI series is often sparse or begins later, limiting how directly the immediate 1979 shock can be verified in the specific charts held.

justification: Capital flight, the oil-export halt, and frozen foreign assets (following the November 1979 hostage crisis) all moved Iran's balance of payments, but in offsetting directions across the current and capital accounts.
caveats: WDI balance-of-payments coverage for Iran in this exact period is thin/irregular, and the asset freeze itself is a US-driven event slightly later than the revolution proper, complicating clean attribution.

justification: Loss of oil-revenue capacity to service debt and the disruption of normal international financial relationships plausibly affected Iran's external-debt trajectory in the years after the revolution.
caveats: Most external-debt series in this index begin only in 1980, right at the boundary of this event, and are further confounded by the war's own borrowing needs from September 1980.

event_title: Oil price collapse begins economic crisis
justification: Although this event is filed under Venezuela's timeline, the underlying trigger (OPEC's Nov 2014 decision not to cut output amid the US shale boom) was a genuine global oil-price shock that hit every oil-exporting economy, including Iran. Oil dominates Iran's goods-export receipts, so the price collapse mechanically reduced export value and narrowed Iran's current-account surplus into 2015-16.
caveats: Iran's exports over this same window were simultaneously constrained by nuclear-related oil sanctions (tight 2012-2015, partially eased under the Jan 2016 JCPOA implementation), which is a much larger and more Iran-specific driver of the same series than the global price move; isolating the pure price effect from the sanctions/volume effect is not possible from the chart alone, and WDI current-account estimates for Iran carry substantial uncertainty.

event_title: Oil price collapse begins economic crisis
justification: Iran's state budget is built around a projected oil-export-revenue line; the global price collapse cut that revenue, pushing the government toward deficit financing that can also feed parallel-market rial depreciation and pressure on the FX gap in subsequent years.
caveats: For Iran specifically, sanctions cycles (tightening 2012-2015, JCPOA relief from 2016, snapback from 2018) and domestic monetary/fiscal policy are far more visible drivers of both the FX gap and nominal budget totals than the global oil-price cycle; nominal rial budget totals can rise even as real oil revenue falls, because of concurrent inflation and currency depreciation, which obscures any clean directional read.

justification: The event describes the worldwide quadrupling of oil prices after the October 1973 Arab oil embargo; as a major OPEC exporter Iran received the same global price shock, and Iran's oil rents (% of GDP) in the WDI series jumped from 18.6% in 1973 to 47.4% in 1974, the single largest one-year jump in that series.
caveats: This record is filed under Venezuela in the source timeline and literally describes Venezuela's own windfall, not an Iranian action; the link here is to the shared underlying global oil-price shock, not to anything Venezuela did. Iran's 1974 ratio also reflects rising production volume, Iran's own push for higher OPEC posted prices, and GDP-denominator effects, not the world price alone.

justification: Russia's 1998 default coincided with a global oil glut (Russia and other producers dumping crude for cash) that helped push world oil prices to roughly $10/barrel in 1998; Iran's oil rents (% of GDP) fell from 18.0% (1997) to 11.6% (1998) in the WDI series over the same window.
caveats: The 1997-98 Asian financial crisis (collapsing regional oil demand) and OPEC's 1997 quota increase are usually cited as the primary drivers of the 1998 price crash; Russia's default is at most a minor contributing factor, and Iran's own 1998 conditions add further confounds.

event_title: Russo-Georgian War and 2008 financial crisis pressure
lag_description: same year into the following year (2008 to 2009)
justification: The global financial crisis named in this event (bundled with the Russo-Georgian War) triggered a collapse in world oil demand and price from about $147 to roughly $40/barrel by year-end; Iran's net barter terms-of-trade index fell from 131.2 (2008) to 99.8 (2009), and its oil rents (% of GDP) fell from 30.6% to 17.6% over the same window.
caveats: The Russo-Georgian War itself had negligible direct effect on Iran; the operative mechanism is the global financial crisis component of this bundled event. The 2008-09 crisis hit nearly every oil exporter simultaneously, so attribution to this specific Russia-tagged event rather than the crisis generally is inherently loose, and Iran's own subsidy/pricing policy also shaped these series.

justification: The event's own description names the collapse in global oil prices as the driver of the ruble's crash; the same worldwide 2014-2016 oil price slide (Brent from ~$115 to ~$35/barrel) pulled Iran's terms-of-trade index down from 134.6 (2014) to 100 (2015) to 91.5 (2016), and Iran's oil rents (% of GDP) from 21.2% to 12.3% over 2014-2015.
caveats: Russia's ruble collapse is a parallel symptom of the same global oil-price shock rather than a cause of Iran's numbers. Iran's 2015-2016 trade figures are further complicated by the January 2016 JCPOA sanctions relief, which raised Iran's export volumes even as global prices fell, muddying the net effect on these series.

event_title: Russia launches full-scale invasion of Ukraine
justification: The invasion and Western sanctions on Russian energy tightened global oil supply and pushed benchmark crude prices sharply higher in 2022; Iran's Heavy crude OPEC-basket spot price rose from $69.80/barrel (2021) to $99.92/barrel (2022), tracking the global benchmark surge.
caveats: Iran's crude price is set largely off global benchmarks (already trading at a sanctions discount before the war), so the pass-through is fairly direct, but the 2022 price rise also reflects post-COVID demand recovery and OPEC+ supply discipline, not the war alone.

event_title: Russia launches full-scale invasion of Ukraine
justification: The same global oil/gas price spike from the war and sanctions on Russian energy lifted Iran's net barter terms-of-trade index from 108.4 (2021) to 131.7 (2022), since oil dominates Iran's export basket.
caveats: Iran's own oil export volumes also grew substantially in 2022 as China absorbed sanctioned Iranian crude at larger scale, an Iran-specific dynamic that separately boosts the terms-of-trade figure alongside the global price effect; the two cannot be disentangled from this series alone.
